title: Depletion of Natural Resources
event: Hollis Co. acquires a coal mine for $2,400,000, expects $300,000 of restoration costs, and estimates a $200,000 residual land value. The mine holds an estimated 1,000,000 tons; 80,000 tons are extracted this year. Prepare the depletion entry.

lines.label: 80,000 tons × $2.50
lines.why: Depletion flows into INVENTORY first — it becomes expense only as the coal sells.
lines.trap: Debiting depletion expense directly skips the inventory stage; unsold coal should hold its depletion on the balance sheet.

lines.label: resource consumed
lines.why: The contra-asset tracks the mine's consumption.
lines.trap: Base = acquisition + restoration − residual: restoration ADDS (a real future cost), residual SUBTRACTS.

memorize.body: Depletion base = acquisition + development + restoration (ARO) − residual value. Rate = base ÷ estimated units; depletion = rate × units EXTRACTED.
Extraction drives depletion into inventory; SALES drive it into COGS. Two different triggers, one period apart when coal stockpiles.

questions.prompt: Base: 2,400,000 + 300,000 restoration − 200,000 residual, over 1,000,000 tons; 80,000 tons extracted. Depletion = ?

distractors.feedback: You dropped the restoration cost from the base (2.40/ton). Restoration is a real cost of the resource: rate = 2.50.
Residual value sign flipped — it SUBTRACTS from the base: (2,400 + 300 − 200)k ÷ 1M = $2.50/ton.

build.scaffold: Build the base (+ restoration, − residual), rate it per ton, apply to tons EXTRACTED, and land it in inventory.
